title: Hygiene and Skin Care for a Dependent Patient: Comfort and Infection Prevention
subtitle: A 76-year-old woman who is dependent and incontinent after a stroke needs total hygiene care. The nurse provides bathing, perineal, and oral care that protects her skin, prevents infection, and preserves her comfort and dignity.

patient.name: Estelle Moreau
patient.admitDate: Rehab unit after a stroke; dependent for all ADLs and incontinent of urine and stool. Sacral and perineal skin reddened from moisture. Requires total hygiene care, frequent skin protection, and oral care.

vitals.temp: Skin breakdown, foul odor, new fever

nursesNote: Estelle Moreau, 76, dependent and incontinent after a stroke, with perineal/sacral redness from moisture. Hygiene care protects comfort, dignity, skin integrity, and prevents infection. Key practices: hand hygiene before/after care; clean-to-dirty bathing; perineal care FRONT TO BACK after each incontinence episode with a moisture barrier; oral care every shift (even if NPO) with aspiration precautions; reposition at least q2h keeping skin clean and dry; distinguish incontinence-associated dermatitis from a pressure injury; and safe linen handling. Avoid scrubbing bony prominences, hot water, leaving skin damp, and placing soiled linens on the floor.
providerOrders: Total hygiene care with skin and oral assessment each shift
Hand hygiene and clean gloves for body-fluid contact
Perineal care front to back after each incontinence episode; apply moisture barrier
Oral care every shift (side-lying with suction if decreased consciousness)
Reposition at least every 2 hours; keep skin clean and dry; contain soiled linens

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What are this patient's hygiene needs and risks?

opts.text: A dependent, incontinent patient is at risk for skin breakdown and infection
Hygiene care supports comfort, dignity, and skin integrity
Hand hygiene is the single most important infection-prevention measure
Oral care is needed even when a patient is NPO
Incontinence needs no special skin care as long as the patient is turned
Moisture from incontinence can cause incontinence-associated dermatitis
Perineal care for a woman is done front to back to reduce UTI risk
A moisture barrier protects skin exposed to urine or stool
Incontinence-associated dermatitis differs from a pressure injury; prevent both
For an unconscious patient, position side-lying with suction during oral care
Bony prominences should be massaged vigorously to improve circulation
Reposition at least every 2 hours and keep the skin clean and dry
Perform hand hygiene, gather supplies, and provide privacy
Assess skin, mouth, and continence needs
Bathe clean to dirty; perform perineal care front to back
Provide oral care and apply a moisture barrier as needed
Reposition, manage linens safely, and document skin status
Perform hand hygiene before and after care
Wash the perineum front to back
Provide oral care even though the patient is NPO
Apply a moisture barrier to skin exposed to incontinence
Bathe from the cleanest area to the dirtiest
Reposition and keep the skin clean and dry
Vigorously scrub a reddened bony prominence
Use hot water and leave the skin damp
Place soiled linens on the floor or shake them

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Don gloves and clean the cleanest areas first, moving clean to dirty
Cleanse the perineum front to back and pat the skin dry
Apply a barrier cream to areas exposed to moisture
Provide oral care safely (side-lying with suction if unconscious)
Reposition, replace soiled linens without shaking, and document
Skin stays clean, dry, and intact
The patient is comfortable and her dignity is maintained
Perineal skin is healthy under barrier protection with no infection
The mouth is moist and clean, lowering aspiration-pneumonia risk
Perineal skin breaks down from prolonged moisture and stool
A sacral pressure injury develops from infrequent repositioning
A UTI develops after back-to-front cleansing
Oral care is skipped and the patient develops aspiration pneumonia

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Basic Care and Comfort, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
